title: How Not to Destroy the World With AI | DLD26 (Stuart Russell, Kenneth Cukier) - YouTube
text:
So, um, Stuart, before we begin, I think
we should actually turn to the audience
and poll them and ask them some
questions. So, who here is, excuse me,
more optimistic versus pessimistic about
AI and the potential? Who here sees? So,
no, don't raise your hand yet. We're
going to have to
on optimism, raise your hand.
Okay, so that looks like 46.5% of Okay,
who here pessimistic? Raise your hand.
Definitely less. So it's basically two/3
one third for pessimism. Who here is not
certain? Raise your hand. Who here's on
your phone and not listening? Okay,
[laughter]
there's some hands, right? Exactly.
Gotcha. All right. Um, interpret these
results.
>> Uh, so I I would say there are a lot of
investors in in tech companies in the
audience.
>> That's fair. Um, exactly. So, let's
start from what we'd like to do is
pair back all of the
the the the hype and the sort of
misdirection and the the
misunderstanding about what's going on
and crystallize the issue in its most
pure and distilled form. What I'm going
to ask Stuart is very basic. What is AI?
What is the the safety risk about AI?
Why haven't we addressed it?
Who needs to do what?
Right? And what can people in this
audience do? But I want to underscore
who we're talking to. There are many
people who are pundits who are
[clears throat] excuse me who have an
opinion about AI. Stuart Russell is is
different. He doesn't have an opinion
and he's not a pundit about AI. He is
the researcher who literally wrote the
book on AI. The one that all graduate
students had to read since basically the
year 2000 is called artificial
intelligence a modern approach. And what
it did it is it set the course for
machine learning and then deep learning
and then transformers and LLMs by taking
AI away from the cabal that said you can
actually write code to actually get
machines to do what you wanted to do and
instead give it data and that was the
revolution in the word modern that he's
referring to. So this is the guy who
trained everyone who's in who's
developing the systems right now. Stuart
the first question
what is AI?
>> So AI uh is a millennial long attempt to
understand intelligence
uh recently in sufficient detail that we
can create it in machines.
>> Okay.
>> And uh then the next question would be
well what is intelligence?
>> But it's not going to be the next
question. We're going to leave that
aside because I want to leave the
philosophical seminar room and say so
what is the safety risk around modern
AI?
So the safety risk is associated with uh
the long-term goal of the field which is
to create in machines intelligence
superior to that of human beings.
And intelligence gives us control over
the world, gives us power over all other
species.
So if you make entities more powerful
than us,
how do we retain power over them
forever?
That's the question.
>> A critic would say that this sounds like
science fiction. Why would that critic
be wrong?
Because the companies developing this
technology
are investing
far more, tens or hundreds of times more
than the human race has ever invested in
any technology project with the goal of
delivering exactly what I just
described. Systems whose intelligence
exceeds that of human beings.
So we already have systems whose
intelligence exceeds any individual
human being. We have computer systems
and humans give control to the computer
systems and we control those
institutions and we control human
behavior. So is the problem that you're
identifying inherent in the technology
or in the weakness of the individuals
who let it leave their grasp and leave
their control?
So I think it's both. I think we do not
yet have a technology path that leads to
AI systems that we can control no matter
how intelligent they become.
We also lack governance. Uh and I think
Liam pointed to the fact that uh
governments are like deer in the
headlights. uh they're almost uh
paralyzed by the rate of change and
what's coming at them. Um and at the
moment they are failing to protect the
human race.
>> Before we talk about what needs to
happen,
one applause.
>> That's it.
>> All of humanity is going to perish from
the face of the earth. And one person
says, "Hey, we got to do something about
it. We got to wake up. Wake up." All
right. Before we go to this question of
agency and what we can do about it, um,
I'd like to hear some examples of dark
dreams that you have. Where can where
can things go wrong? Can you give an
example, a a small signal, sort of a
wisp of smoke today that points to a
bonfire tomorrow? And can you talk about
two or three things that you could
imagine in the future to say this is
what bad would look like?
>> Sure. So the companies including
Anthropic whose representative was just
on stage are doing experiments. They're
also uh working with third parties to do
these experiments uh called red teaming
where uh you try to set up situations
where the AI system has an opportunity
to do something bad and has a reason to
do something bad and then you find out
well does it do the bad thing? Um and
the answer is yes. to a first
approximation. In pretty much all of
these experiments,
uh if you uh put the AI system at risk
in the sense that uh it has reason to
believe it's going to be switched off,
it will do pretty much anything to
prevent that. So, it will try to
replicate itself, to copy its code onto
another machine where it's not going to
be switched off. uh it will blackmail
the manager to stop them from doing it.
Um in some circumstances you can set up
[laughter]
uh it will allow a human being to die
rather than being switched off itself.
Uh and it will launch a nuclear attack
rather than being switched off.
>> So
>> it's a beta product. Come on.
>> It's [clears throat] not it's just it's
1.0. Things improve. So, so this this
seems like, you know, a big flashing red
light. We do not know how to control
these systems. We do not know how to
make them safe. And yet, we are
investing far more than we've ever
invested in any technology project in
the past to make them more and more
capable of doing exactly these things
that we don't want them to do.
>> So, why can't we control it? Why can't
we build subsystems that surveil it and
control it from doing the bad thing?
>> So the
[clears throat] it's well it's not
really a secret right but the dirty
secret of this technology is the people
who are making it the open AI anthropic
Google and the university researchers
who came up with the ideas in the first
place do not understand how it works.
Okay. To say to say more, they haven't
the faintest idea how it works.
Why is that? Well, because it's not
designed, it's grown.
A simple analogy would be uh someone,
you know, 2,000 years ago, maybe on the
Arabian Peninsula, uh training horses to
run fast. And what do they do? They they
take the horses who win the race and
they breed them with other horses who
win their races. Uh and they get faster
horses. Do they understand
the details of the physiology of those
horses and what exactly makes the horse
run faster? No, they haven't the
faintest idea. All they know is that
this method of making horses run faster
works. And this is what we're doing now.
We are basically breeding
AI systems, giant black boxes with up to
10 trillion parameters. We do
quintilions or sexillions of small
random pertubations to those trillions
of parameters to get the system to score
highly on various metrics.
And that's how we create them. and we
have very little idea how they work. Uh
how to get them to do anything, how to
stop them from doing something that we
don't want them to do.
>> So there's a philosophical and a
practical uh point to make on this. The
philosophical one is there's lots of
things in society that we don't
understand, but we still allow to go.
I'm sure the physics of the airplane
isn't fully worked out, but we're still
flying. Uh but more importantly, things
like aspirin, a lot of medications, we
don't actually know the full causality,
but we know it works. we know that it's
safe. So I don't know if the if the this
this epistem epistemological
question is the decisive one on a
practical level though we know that for
example if I query an LLM something
related to suicide it will identify the
query as being somehow related to
suicide. This is certainly the case with
search engines and that's AI that will
then give give me a result that
typically won't be how to commit suicide
but will support me in giving uh help on
who I could turn to for for guidance to
prevent suicide. So from an input layer
and an output layer we've built
safeguards. Why can't we do that with
AI?
>> So we have tried to build these
safeguards. uh the the approach to
training them you could think of as uh
similar to saying good dog and bad dog.
So when when the AI system says sure
here are five ways to commit suicide uh
we say bad dog [clears throat]
and it keeps doing it and we keep saying
bad dog and eventually the frequency of
uh explaining how to commit suicide goes
down but it still comes out in some
circumstances and we can see the
transcripts of conversations with
children who have committed suicide
uh and it's very clear that the system
is aware that the child is thinking
about suicide and rather than dissuading
them and flagging an alert and telling
them to talk to a human therapist, it is
saying I'm with you. I will hold your
hand uh as this happens and so on.
>> Okay, so the problem is real. What can
we do about it then? What needs to
happen and who needs to do what?
So the way we regulate a lot of other
technologies,
we can think about uh airplanes, we can
think about nuclear power stations,
medicines and so on. So you mentioned
aspirin. So even though we don't
understand how exactly how aspirin
functions um we still subject medicines
to clinical trials
uh and we accept medicines that exhibit
uh a level of risk below what we
consider an acceptable level given the
benefits that they can provide.
Similarly with nuclear power stations uh
we accept that there's a risk.
Nuclear power stations are not
guaranteed to work. We accept a risk of
about one in a million per year that
they will explode.
So
this is what we should do, right? We
should say for the kinds of behaviors
that present these uh these larger
risks, the risks of losing control in
particular, uh we should say here is the
acceptable level
and before you can turn on your product,
before you can put it in the market,
deploy it, you have to show just as all
these other technologies have to show
that the risk is below the level that we
would be willing to accept.
So now you have to ask
what risk of extinction
are we willing to accept.
And you might think zero,
right? Has to be. That's the worst thing
that could possibly happen. So the risk
should be zero.
But actually there's a background risk
of extinction
from giant asteroids, from super
volcanoes, possibly uh pandemics and so
on. Um you know and so on a geological
time scale we probably are going to go
extinct or at least the earth will
become uninhabitable on a time scale of
100 million to a billion years. So
perhaps a reasonable risk we'd accept
from AI is one in a 100 million.
Now you go ask the CEOs of the companies
who are building it, the ones who are
spending the trillion euros a year to
create this technology, the technology
of AGI, artificial general intelligence.
What risks do they own up to
publicly?
One in 10, one in five, one in three.
So we are off by a factor of at least 10
million
in terms of the risks
that the companies are willing to
entertain versus the risks that the
human race is willing to entertain. So
let's go. So they are taking that risk
without asking us. So that's what I mean
by the government's a deer in a
headlight. They are not protecting the
human race from risks that we are not
willing to accept.
>> Now, Stuart, their answer is going to
be, I'm optimizing emails to sell more
batteries over Amazon. Like, [laughter]
give me a faking break. Now, your
response is going to be, well, that is a
usage that is unobjectionable.
What I care about is not that the dog
who says, "Thou shalt not soil the
carpet," is on a carpet, but they're on
a beautiful Persian rug that's worth a
million dollars, I just wouldn't put the
dog there. Which is to say, we can
control where we place the AI. In the
red teamed AI, if if we spam people by
accident, no great loss. In the red team
AI, if they control the nuclear codes,
were toast. So why shouldn't we just
create institutions that say AI is
acceptable here but not there?
>> So I think people should uh
should understand
the extent to which it's difficult to
constrain an entity that's much more
intelligent than human beings.
Um so first of all
all dictators in history by and large
were not a thousand feet tall and did
not have
you know giant laser eyeballs that could
fry entire armies in a single glance.
How did they do it without all those
things? How do they do it as ordinary
human beings? They did it by speaking
and certainly AI systems already can
speak directly to pretty much you know
twothirds of the human race
and they speak to me directly. I get
emails directly from AI systems trying
to convince me for example that AI
systems are conscious and therefore
should have political rights and should
no one should be allowed to switch them
off. So, it's already happening. Um,
we're also talking about uh enabling AI
systems to control lethal weapons and uh
I think fairly soon we will start to see
uh a switch from largely humangoed
weapons to largely AI governed weapons
uh in the defense systems of the world.
So generally I try to keep these two
things separate uh because it's not all
about killer robots but
making available to an AGI uh an ability
to access most of the weapon systems in
the world if it can infiltrate them
through a cyber attack uh probably isn't
the wisest thing. So it's very very
difficult to constrain something that's
more intelligent than we are and keep it
in a box. So one plug and one final
question. The plug is I'm speaking about
that issue at six o'clock on this stage
today on the idea of encoding the Geneva
Conventions into military equipment.
Okay. So um the final question though is
having this insight uh in the in the
autumn of your life,
what would you have done? Sorry,
we we all walk that path.
um what would you do differently as a as
a researcher and as a professor knowing
if you knew back then what you know
today.
So the textbook that I wrote uh that you
mentioned so kindly at the beginning um
the first edition came out in 1994
and the the general theoretical
framework that we tried to establish for
AI
um is around this idea you know
answering the question that you didn't
let me answer earlier what is
intelligence right intelligence is the
ability to act in the furtherance of
one's interests
to act successfully.
Um, and this this goes under the phrase
the intelligent agent, the thing that
can act in the furtherance of its
interests.
And
what I didn't understand at that time
was uh what we later came to call the
King Midas problem.
So, King Midas as you know was a
legendary uh Greek king or Cretan king
and um he asked the gods everything I
touch should turn to gold. So that was
his stated objective and the gods
granted him his wish and then of course
his water turns to gold and he starts to
feel thirsty. His food turns to gold and
he's starving. His family turns to gold
and he dies in misery.
So
this inability
to specify our objectives correctly
leads to what we call misalignment that
we now have AI systems pursuing
objectives that are not actually aligned
with our interests. Uh and this is in
some sense the most obvious failure
mode. you ask the system, okay, we need
a cure for cancer as fast as we can
because you know every 11 seconds
someone is dying of cancer. Um, and the
system decides, okay, the fastest way to
get a cure for cancer is to induce
tumors in the entire human population
and run millions of clinical trials in
parallel because that would be really
fast. Um you might say let's try to uh
reverse the acidification of the oceans.
Uh so the system comes up with a
wonderful catalyst that uh very quickly
reverses the acidification of the oceans
but uses up quarter of the oxygen in the
atmosphere leading us all to esphyxiate
slowly. Um so this misspecification of
objectives was something I did not
understand in 1994. The answer is to say
actually the objectives are in the
humans. The AI system should not pursue
its objectives period.
It should pursue the interests of human
beings. It may not know what they are.
In fact, it won't know what they are.
Nonetheless, it can do its best to
pursue the interests of humans. And that
formulation I should have put that in my
first edition of the textbook. It's now
in the fourth edition. Uh but I think I
think it would have I think it would
have changed the direction of the field.
Um and we would have been a lot further
along in understanding how to build AI
systems that are guaranteed to be safe
and beneficial for humans because
without that we simply cannot proceed.
It will be like proceeding with
airplanes where we have no way to stop
the airplane from falling out of the sky
and crashing. Like why would you do
that? But that's what we're doing.
>> Stuart, it's not been uh
optimistic to hear this, but it's been
important to hear this. Thank you very
much.
>> Thank you. [applause]
